title: Dog Knee Clicking: Causes and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Is your dog's knee clicking? Learn why this happens, if it's an emergency, and how to help your pup with our veterinary triage guide.
content_html:
TL;DR: A clicking sound in a dog's knee is typically caused by issues like patellar luxation or a meniscus tear and is usually a low-urgency condition. Unless your dog is in extreme distress or unable to walk, you can monitor them and schedule a veterinary exam within a few days.

Why is my dog's knee making a clicking or popping sound?

Hearing a clicking or popping sound coming from your dog's knee can be concerning for any pet owner. This sound, often medically referred to as crepitus, typically occurs when the kneecap (patella) slips out of its groove or when there is a tear in the meniscus, which is the cartilage that cushions the knee joint. It is a common symptom associated with conditions like patellar luxation or a cranial cruciate ligament (CCL) injury.

Is a clicking sound in my dog's knee a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for a clicking knee is generally considered Low and is rarely a medical emergency.
  • Seek immediate attention only if the sound is accompanied by extreme distress or a total inability to walk.
  • Monitor your dog closely for signs of lameness or an inability to put weight on the affected leg.
  • If your dog seems comfortable otherwise, schedule a regular appointment with your veterinarian for an orthopedic exam within the next few days.

How can videos and photos help my veterinarian diagnose my dog's knee issue?

  • Provide your veterinarian with a video of your dog walking or a photo of how they sit to capture their natural posture.
  • Record the specific "skipping" motion or the clicking sound itself to help the vet see the issue clearly outside of a clinical setting.
  • Visual evidence is vital because dogs often hide symptoms or walk differently at the clinic due to excitement, adrenaline, or stress.
  • A clear home recording can lead to a faster and more accurate diagnosis by showing exactly what is happening in a relaxed environment.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

A clicking sound during locomotion or flexion and extension of the stifle joint in dogs may indicate a medial meniscal injury, often associated with cranial cruciate ligament tears. Patellar luxation, where the patella displaces from the trochlear groove, can also cause stifle joint clicking; medial luxations are more common in cats and small dog breeds, while lateral luxations are more frequent in large dogs. Clinical signs of patellar luxation vary with severity, ranging from mild, infrequent signs in Grade I to severe lameness and limb deformities in Grade IV. Palpation of the stifle joint can reveal patellar displacement, and radiography can reveal limb changes. If a clicking sound is noted in the stifle joint, veterinary examination is warranted to determine the underlying cause and appropriate treatment.

Chapter: Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1199)

faqs.question: Can a clicking knee heal on its own?
faqs.answer: Structural issues like a luxating patella or a torn ligament typically do not heal without veterinary intervention, though inflammation may temporarily subside with rest.

faqs.question: Should I stop my dog from jumping?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is highly recommended to limit high-impact activities like jumping, running, or climbing stairs until a vet has evaluated the joint to prevent further damage.

faqs.question: Does clicking always mean my dog is in pain?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Some dogs may not show obvious signs of pain, but the clicking indicates mechanical friction that can lead to chronic arthritis if left untreated.
